description: Scotland's largest city — a gritty, creative powerhouse of Victorian architecture, world-class museums, and remarkable street art.

pois.name: George Square
pois.summary: Glasgow's grand civic heart, ringed by Victorian architecture and overlooked by the City Chambers — and one of Europe's most used film locations, having doubled for Philadelphia, San Francisco, and various American cities across dozens of productions.

trivia.title: World War Z (2013)
trivia.body: George Square doubled as downtown Philadelphia in Brad Pitt's zombie blockbuster. The production closed the city centre for several days and transformed the square with American street furniture, yellow cabs, and debris. Glasgow is one of the most used stand-in cities in the world — its Victorian streetscape reads as almost any Anglo-American city from above.

trivia.title: The Battle of George Square
trivia.body: On 31 January 1919, a huge crowd of striking workers gathered here demanding a 40-hour working week. The government, fearing a Bolshevik-style revolution, deployed 10,000 English troops and tanks to Glasgow — notably not Scottish troops, whom they didn't trust to fire on the crowd. The event became known as Bloody Friday and is a defining moment in Scottish labour history.

trivia.title: Fast & Furious 6, Cloud Atlas & more
trivia.body: Glasgow is one of Europe's most used film stand-ins. Its grid of Victorian streets has doubled for Philadelphia (World War Z), San Francisco (Cloud Atlas), and various American cities across dozens of productions. Fast & Furious 6 (2013) filmed chase sequences on the Kingston Bridge and surrounding streets. The 2019 war epic 1917 used the former Govan docks as a WWI canal crossing. Directors love Glasgow because it offers century-old architecture without the tourist crowds of London.

trivia.title: Glasgow University as medieval England
trivia.body: Glasgow's Victorian Gothic University, just north of the city centre, doubled as the English royal court in Mel Gibson's Braveheart (1995). King Edward II practised archery among its ornate cloisters, and the medieval banquet scene with the swan was filmed in the same quadrangle. The university's 19th-century architecture reads convincingly as medieval on screen — the same trick used when the BBC filmed parts of Ivanhoe here decades earlier.

pois.name: Kelvingrove Art Gallery
pois.summary: One of Scotland's most visited attractions — a magnificent red sandstone palace housing Salvador Dalí's Christ of Saint John of the Cross, a world-class arms and armour collection, and a stuffed elephant named Sir Roger. Free entry, and genuinely unmissable.

trivia.title: Built the wrong way round
trivia.body: Local legend holds that the architect was so horrified when he realised he'd built the museum back-to-front — with the grand entrance facing away from the main road — that he threw himself from one of its towers. This is entirely false: the building was deliberately designed so the 'rear' faced the grand boulevard, and the architect lived happily for many years after it opened in 1901.

trivia.title: Dalí's Christ
trivia.body: The gallery's most famous work, Salvador Dalí's Christ of Saint John of the Cross (1951), shows the crucifixion from an aerial perspective above Christ's head, with a Scottish fishing village below. Glasgow Corporation bought it for £8,200 in 1952 — a decision widely mocked at the time. It was damaged by a visitor who threw a stone at it in 1961. It is now worth an estimated £80 million.

trivia.title: Taggart — Glasgow on screen
trivia.body: While Inspector Rebus stalks Edinburgh, Glasgow has its own crime fiction identity through Taggart — the longest-running TV detective series in the world, airing from 1983 to 2010. The show used real Glasgow locations obsessively: the Necropolis, the Clyde, Kelvingrove, the West End tenements. Its catchphrase 'There's been a murder' became so associated with the city that Glaswegians began saying it ironically at the slightest inconvenience. The show attracted 12 million UK viewers at its peak and ran for 27 years.

pois.name: Glasgow School of Art
pois.summary: Charles Rennie Mackintosh's masterpiece — a building so revolutionary it changed the course of European design. Twice devastated by fire, it is currently being painstakingly restored by craftspeople having to rediscover lost 19th-century techniques.

trivia.title: Mackintosh's genius
trivia.body: Charles Rennie Mackintosh designed this building in 1896 at just 28 years old, and it was constructed in two phases over 13 years. The design blends Scottish baronial, Art Nouveau, and a strange proto-modernism that was decades ahead of its time. Mackintosh died in 1928, largely forgotten, having sold his furniture for almost nothing. He is now recognised as one of the most influential architects of the 20th century.

trivia.title: Destroyed twice by fire
trivia.body: The Mackintosh building was devastated by fire in May 2014 during student works, destroying the famous library wing. Before restoration was complete, a second, far more destructive fire in June 2018 gutted almost the entire building. The cause remains disputed. Restoration is ongoing and enormously complex — craftspeople are having to rediscover lost techniques to recreate the original interiors.

pois.name: Glasgow Necropolis
pois.summary: A Victorian garden cemetery on a dramatic hill overlooking the city, with over 50,000 burials and extraordinary monuments. The views over Glasgow from the top are among the finest in the city, and the stories buried here range from the tragic to the genuinely bizarre.

trivia.title: The stalker of the Southern Necropolis
trivia.body: In 1954, hundreds of children armed with sharpened sticks and knives descended on the Southern Necropolis (a separate cemetery in the Gorbals) after rumours spread of a '7-foot tall vampire with iron teeth' who had abducted and eaten two local boys. No children were missing, and police found no evidence of any vampire. The panic was blamed on American horror comics — and led directly to the Children and Young Persons (Harmful Publications) Act 1955.

trivia.title: Banishment to Glasgow
trivia.body: In 1585, a tailor named David Duly was convicted of failing to report his wife's death from plague to Edinburgh's authorities. He was sentenced to hang outside his own house, but the rope broke. Taking this as divine intervention, the court commuted his sentence — to banishment to Glasgow. At the time, this was apparently considered only marginally better than death.

pois.name: River Clyde & Riverside Museum
pois.summary: The river that built Glasgow's industrial empire — at its peak, one in five of the world's ships was built on these banks. Zaha Hadid's striking Riverside Museum now stands where the ships were launched, housing one of the finest transport collections in Europe.

trivia.title: What is the Nile to the mighty Clyde?
trivia.body: At the height of the British Empire, Glasgow's shipyards on the Clyde produced more ships than any other place on earth — at their peak in the early 20th century, one in five of the world's ships was built here. When Grand Duke Alexis of Russia visited the John Elder yard in Govan, he famously described Glasgow as 'the centre of the intelligence of England' — a geographical confusion that presumably did not delight his Scottish hosts.

trivia.title: The Ghostly Armies of the Clyde
trivia.body: In the summer of 1686, many witnesses in the Clyde Valley reported extraordinary visions: showers of bonnets, hats, guns, and swords falling from the sky, covering the trees and ground; companies of men in arms marching along the riverside; armies meeting armies and dissolving into the ground. Covenanting memoirist Patrick Walker recorded the accounts in detail, noting that even hardened sceptics present were visibly shaken.
